coredata.dc:title: Non-cytotoxic fluorescent wood for selective detection and efficient removal of tetracycline
coredata.dc:creator: Qi, Xinmiao
Chen, Yizheng
Peng, Junwen
Zhang, Xuefeng
Qiu, Xinyi
Li, Xianjun
Xie, Xiangjing
Guo, Xin
coredata.dc:description: The antibiotic contamination in water is a growing environmental concern, which prompts the need for an efficient and nontoxic adsorbent to eliminate antibiotics from water. Tetracycline (TC) is a widely used antibiotic owing to its low cost and high effectiveness, which adds to significant environmental impact. In this paper, we present a novel non-cytotoxic fluorescent wood (NCFW) with remarkable properties, including a high TC adsorption capacity (up to 99.17 mg/g), good selectivity and sensitivity for TC detection, favorable fluorescence (absolute quantum yield of 3.9%), and noncytotoxicity (cell viability of more than 90%). Our results revealed the unique structure of the NCFW as a framework for improving the adsorption capacity, whereas the fluorescent probes can act as effective sensors for detecting TC. These findings indicate that the NCFW has the potential to be a promising adsorbent for removing TC from wastewater.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Wood
Adsorption
Tetracycline

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 31890772 ), Changsha Natural Science Foundation (Grant No. kq2208414), Hunan Youth Science and Technology Innovation Talent Project (Grant No. 2020RC3043), Training Plan of Young Backbone Teachers in Colleges and Universities of Hunan Province, China (Grant No. 71), General Project by the Department of Education of Hunan Province (Grant No. 21C056) and Hunan Institute of Engineering Talent Research Project (Grant No. 09001003-22013). The authors would like to thank Jialing Wu from Shiyanjia Lab (www.shiyanjia.com) for the XPS analysis.

This work was supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 31890772), Changsha Natural Science Foundation (Grant No. kq2208414), Hunan Youth Science and Technology Innovation Talent Project (Grant No. 2020RC3043), Training Plan of Young Backbone Teachers in Colleges and Universities of Hunan Province, China (Grant No. 71), General Project by the Department of Education of Hunan Province (Grant No. 21C056) and Hunan Institute of Engineering Talent Research Project (Grant No. 09001003-22013). The authors would like to thank Jialing Wu from Shiyanjia Lab (www.shiyanjia.com) for the XPS analysis.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: A visual representation in a schematic format.

(a-c) SEM images of UW, DW, and NCFW-4. (d) XRD analysis. (e) FTIR patterns. (f) UV–vis and PL spectra. (g) The behavior of fluorescence dependent by the excitation wavelength. (h) Various NCFWs showed distinguishable blue characteristics corresponding with various concentrations of CDs. (i-g) NCFW's radial (R) and longitudinal (L) cross-sectional views.

(a-c) 3D surface plots for interactions. (d-e) The influence of pH and temperature (Experimental conditions: m = 0.3 g, V = 100 mL, C0 = 60 mg/L, t = 24 h). (f-g) Sorption isotherms (Experimental conditions: m = 0.3 g, V = 100 mL, pH = 8, t = 24 h, and T = 20℃) and kinetics (Experimental conditions: m = 0.3 g, V = 100 mL, C0 = 60 mg/L, pH = 8, and T = 20℃) onto UW, DW and NCFW-4. (h-i) Non-linear models including Langmuir and Freundlich. (j-k) Non-linear models including pseudo-first-order and pseudo-second-order.
Reusability studies. (a) Recycling tests for the adsorption–desorption of NCFW-4. (b-c) FTIR and XRD spectra of NCFW-4 before and after 5 consecutive cycles.

(a) Fluorescence quenching of NCFW-4 was examined in the presence of various antibiotics at a concentration of 2000 mg/L. (b) The AQY of NCFW-4 was determined after the introduction of different antibiotics.

(a) Fluorescence quenching and AQY of NCFW-4 for different TC concentrations. (b) Fluorescent spectra of NCFW-4 for different TC concentrations and plots of the values of F0/F − 1 versus concentration.
(a) NIH/3T3 Cell viability was evaluated using UW and NCFW-4. (b) “Live-Dead” fluorescent imaging, where blue and red fluorescence corresponded to live and dead cells, respectively.
FTIR and XPS analysis of NCFW-4. (a) FTIR analysis, (b) XPS survey of NCFW-4, (c-d) O1s and N1s spectra.
(a) UV–vis spectrum of TC and excitation spectrum of NCFW-4. (b) Fluorescence decay curves of NCFW-4 and NCFW-4 adsorbed by TC. (c–d) Fitting curves.
A schematic representation of the adsorption and detection mechanism involved in the studied system.
Maximum sorption capacities in comparison with previous work.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Comparison of different fluorescent probes for the TC detection.

row.entry: Thioglycolic acid capped CdS quantum dots
An europium functionalized ZnO quantum dots

head.ce:title: Non-cytotoxic fluorescent wood for selective detection and efficient removal of tetracycline

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: The authors equally contribute to manuscript.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The antibiotic contamination in water is a growing environmental concern, which prompts the need for an efficient and nontoxic adsorbent to eliminate antibiotics from water. Tetracycline (TC) is a widely used antibiotic owing to its low cost and high effectiveness, which adds to significant environmental impact. In this paper, we present a novel non-cytotoxic fluorescent wood (NCFW) with remarkable properties, including a high TC adsorption capacity (up to 99.17 mg/g), good selectivity and sensitivity for TC detection, favorable fluorescence (absolute quantum yield of 3.9%), and noncytotoxicity (cell viability of more than 90%). Our results revealed the unique structure of the NCFW as a framework for improving the adsorption capacity, whereas the fluorescent probes can act as effective sensors for detecting TC. These findings indicate that the NCFW has the potential to be a promising adsorbent for removing TC from wastewater.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Antibiotics have emerged as a significant class of pollutants with far-reaching implications for both the environment and human health. The discharge of antibiotics into water can increase the prevalence of resistant microbes and genes, thereby posing potential risks to the environmental and public health [1,2]. Tetracycline (TC) is a commonly used and highly effective antibiotic in the medical field [3]. To address this issue, various techniques have been developed to eliminate antibiotics from water, including ozonation [4], microbial degradation, membrane separation [5], photocatalytic degradation [6–8], and adsorption [9]. Adsorption is considered a promising technique owing to its high efficiency, cost-effectiveness, simplicity, and practicality [10]. However, it is crucial to explore adsorbents that are highly effective, ecologically friendly, and non-toxic to enhance the efficiency of adsorption.

As a naturally occurring and sustainable material, wood has extensive use in various fields, including load-bearing structures, construction, and ornamentation. Recently, the availability and security of wood as a natural resource for environmental applications have piqued the interest of researchers [11]. Some researchers have sought to explore the potential of wood-derived nanofibers as a promising solution. Gomez-Maldonado et al. prepared wood-based cellulose nanofibrils-Tara Gum composite hydrogel with improved performance on dye adsorption [12]. Zhang et al. synthesized wood-based cellulose nanofibril aerogels for the adsorption of phenol pollutants [13]. Despite the encouraging outcomes of studies on wood-derived nanofibers, the preparation process is associated with specific equipment requirements and high energy consumption, which present significant challenges for scalability and commercialization. To address these limitations, delignification combined with in situ modification or direct wood utilization has been developed as an alternative approach. This method employs the natural skeleton of wood-based cellulosic fibers without complete destruction of its original structure; thereby providing a sustainable and environment-friendly option for the production of nanocellulose with the potential for large-scale application. This strategy has prompted the fabrication of high-performance adsorbents derived from wood, such as wood sponges for the efficient removal of Cr(VI) ions [14], amino-functionalized wood flour for high-capacity Pb(II) adsorption [15] and delignified wood supported MIL-100 (Fe) for the rapid adsorption of TC [16]. The progress in this field highlights the capability of direct wood utilization as a promising approach for the production of highly efficient adsorbents.

Carbon dots (CDs) are a class of fluorescent nanomaterials that have gained substantial attention in recent years because of their remarkable optical properties, excellent biocompatibility, and non-toxicity [17]. Several investigations have demonstrated the potential of CDs as a promising tool for the fluorescent detection of antibiotics [18], as they exhibit intense fluorescence upon binding with antibiotics, rendering them useful for the detection of antibiotics [19]. However, few studies have explored the synergistic integration of wood and CDs, and its practical application in the removal of TC from water.

This study aims to develop a novel non-cytotoxic fluorescent wood (NCFW) using wood and CDs to produce an adsorbent for both the efficient removal and fluorescent sensing of TC. The chemical characteristics of the NCFW were analyzed, and its capacity to adsorb and detect TC was assessed. The cytotoxicity of the NCFW was also studied. Furthermore, a mechanistic analysis was carried out to examine the high sensitivity detection capabilities and adsorption efficacy of the NCFW in adsorbing TC.

Cunninghamia lanceolata (Lamb.) Hook. was chosen as the wood of interest in this study. Untreated wood (UW) samples were obtained from the main stem. A Soxhlet extractor was employed using a 2:1 volumetric ratio blend of benzene and alcohol for 48 h. The sample was then thoroughly washed with deionized water to ensure the complete removal of the solvents. The delignification solution was prepared by dissolving 5.77 g sodium chlorite in 100 mL deionized water, and the pH was adjusted to 4.7 using acetic acid. Approximately 3.0 g wood sample underwent delignification by immersion in the delignification solution at 90 °C for 36 h, which was repeated six times, and subsequently flushed with deionized water. The sample was then subjected to H2O2 solution (5 mol/L) and heated at 90 °C for 1 h. Finally, the sample was flushed with deionized water and suspended in ethanol for three days. Hereafter, the resulting sample was referred to as “DW”.

First, 0.84 g citric acid (CA) and 1.07 mL ethylenediamine (EDA) were dissolved in 20 mL deionized water. The resulting solution was placed in a Teflon-lined autoclave with a capacity of 30 mL and heated to 200 °C for 5 h. The product was then cooled to 20 °C and subsequently subjected to dialysis for 5 days to ensure its purity. The transmission electron microscopy (TEM) image of the CDs was provided in Fig. S1.

In this experiment, the DW sample was subjected to 10 mL CDs solution with varying concentrations (0.0002, 0.0006, 0.0010, 0.0014, 0.0018, 0.0022, 0.0026 and 0.0030 wt%) through vacuum impregnation. The impregnation process was repeated five times with each cycle lasting for 30 min to ensure the penetration of the CDs solution. The sample was then rinsed thoroughly with deionized water. To facilitate the penetration of the gel solution, a sample with 5 g acrylic acid (AA), 0.005 g potassium persulfate (PPS), 11 mL NaOH solution (5 mol/L), and 0.06 g N’,N-methylenebisacrylamide (MBA) was subjected to vacuum impregnation. The vacuum was maintained for 30 min, and the process was repeated five times. Subsequently, the sample was heated to 60℃ for 12 h to produce NCFW. After the NCFW was rinsed with deionized water, it was kept in deionized water for three days. The NCFW samples were labeled NCFW-1, NCFW-2, NCFW-3, NCFW-4, NCFW-5, NCFW-6, NCFW-7, and NCFW-8, respectively, based on the concentration of CDs solution used in the treatment process.

The TC adsorption capacity of the prepared sample was evaluated through a series of experiments [20]. The study focused on the influence of three parameters, namely pH, contact time, and initial TC concentration. The effect of pH on the adsorption capacity was examined by varying the pH of the solution in the range of 2–9, while maintaining a constant contact time of at 24 h, initial TC concentration of 60 mg/L, and temperature of 20 °C. To investigate the effect of contact time, it was varied in the range of 30–240 min, while other parameters were kept constant; pH of 8, initial TC concentration of 60 mg/L, and temperature of 20 °C. Furthermore, the effect of the initial TC concentration was studied by maintaining a pH of 8, contact time of 24 h, and temperature of 20 °C, while varying the initial TC concentration from 20 to 160 mg/L. The measurements were performed in triplicate, and the data reported represent the average of the replicates.

The surface morphology of the prepared samples was examined using scanning electron microscopy (SEM) with a Hitachi S-4800 (Japan). The crystalline structure of the samples was analyzed using X-ray diffraction (XRD) on a Bruker D8 Advance (UK). Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) on a Thermo Fisher IN 10TM (USA) was employed to identify the functional groups in the samples. The elemental composition of the samples was determined using X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) with a Thermo Fisher K-Alpha 1063 (UK). Photoluminescence (PL) emission spectra were characterized using a fluorescence spectrophotometer (Edinburgh FLS 9800, UK). The TC concentration was measured using a UV–vis spectrophotometer (Shimadzu UV-2550, Japan) at a wavelength (λmax) of 357 nm. The details for the measurement of the absolute quantum yield (AQY) were presented in the Supporting Material.

Fig. 1 shows the preparation of the NCFW, whereby UW and CDs were employed as primary raw materials. The delignification process was utilized to eliminate most of the lignin and hemicellulose contents, thereby exposing additional sorption sites [21]. Simultaneously, the original three-dimensional (3D) porous structure was loosened and partially disrupted, which increased the surface area. In the first step of vacuum impregnation, water-soluble CDs were uniformly dispersed into the partially disrupted 3D porous structure. Subsequently, the gel solution of monomer AA, crosslinker MBA, and initiator PPS was infused into the partially disrupted 3D porous structure to construct the NCFW.

The microstructural features of the cross-sectional samples of the UW, DW, and NCFW-4 were observed. The presence of a 3D porous configuration in the UW is depicted in Fig. 2 a. Thus, the delignification process had a significant impact on the porous structure of the DW. As revealed in Fig. 2b, this process led to a substantial expansion of the porous structure, thereby replacing the dense cell corners with loosely spaced gaps between the cell walls. The microstructure of the NCFW-4 was characterized by a reconstructed 3D porous structure, as depicted in Fig. 2c. After vacuum impregnation and free-radical polymerization, the reconstructed 3D porous structures in the NCFW-4 were formed, and the microstructural features of the NCFW-4 differed from those of the UW and DW.

Fig. 2d illustrates the XRD patterns of the UW, DW, and NCFW-4. The examination of the XRD patterns revealed the presence of two characteristic peaks at 22.4° and 16.2°, which were linked to the crystal structures of cellulose I [22]. Meanwhile, characteristic peaks associated with cellulose II were not observed [23], suggesting that the cellulose framework was not affected by the delignification and free-radical polymerization procedures. The crystalline index (CrI) was evaluated using the Segal method [24]. The UW, DW, and NCFW-4 had CrI values of 1.76, 1.86, and 1.99, respectively. The higher CrI value of DW than that of UW can be ascribed to the elimination of the lignin matrix during the delignification process [25]. Furthermore, the highest CrI value of the NCFW-4 was ascribed to the reconstruction of the 3D porous structure [26].

Fig. 2e exhibits the FTIR spectra of the UW, DW, and NCFW-4 samples. The FTIR spectrum of the UW revealed two peaks at 1592 and 1505 cm−1, which indicated the aromatic ring in lignin [27], and a peak at 1050 cm−1, which was associated with the C–O stretching and ring vibrations in cellulose species [28]. The absence of the peaks at 1592 and 1505 cm−1 in the FTIR spectrum of DW implied the successful removal of lignin during the delignification process [29]. In addition, the FTIR spectrum of NCFW-4 displayed a peak at 1652 cm−1 indicating the presence of –CO–NH– groups [30], thereby confirming the successful free-radical polymerization using MBA as the linking agent. Furthermore, the reduced intensity of the peak at 1050 cm−1 in the FTIR spectrum of NCFW-4 suggested the participation of the cellulose skeleton in the free-radical polymerization [28].

Fig. 2f presents the UV–vis absorption spectrum of the NCFW-4, which manifests an absorption band at 324 nm, thereby validating the presence of CDs. Moreover, the photoluminescence (PL) spectra of NCFW-4 had an optimum excitation wavelength of 379 nm and maximum emission wavelength of 470 nm. The emission spectra of NCFW-4 at different excitation wavelengths are illustrated in Fig. 2g. The findings indicated a shift toward higher wavelengths along with the excitation wavelengths, which was attributed to the impact of the surface states on the bandgap of the CDs. The surface state was analogous to the molecular state, whereas the size effect was attributed to the quantum dimensions, which contributed to the complexity of the excited states of the CDs [31,32].

Fig. 2h illustrates the fluorescence properties of the synthesized NCFWs. All the NCFWs exhibited blue fluorescence when exposed to UV irradiation at 365 nm. The fluorescent luminosity of the NCFWs initially increased and then decreased with the increase in the CDs content. Among the evaluated NCFWs, NCFW-4 had the highest fluorescent brightness and AQY of 3.9%. The cross-sectional morphology of NCFW-4 was subjected to further analysis, as depicted in Fig. 2i and 2j. The results revealed the uniform distribution of CDs throughout the structure of NCFW-4 with consistent brightness in its interior and exterior [26].
This study investigated the influence of three parameters, namely pH, equilibrium concentration (Ce), and temperature (T) on the TC adsorption capacity (Qe) of NCFW-4, as illustrated in Fig. 3 a–c.

The correlation between the pH and Qe of NCFW-4 toward TC is shown in Fig. 3d. The adsorption capacity of NCFW-4 initially increased with the increase in pH from 2 to 8, followed by a decrease as the pH was further increased to 9. This behavior was found to be closely associated with the charge carried by NCFW-4 and TC. The point of zero charge (PZC) of NCFW-4 was determined to be 8.6, as shown in Fig. S2. Meanwhile, the charge of TC was positive at pH values below 3.3, neutral at pH values of 3.3–7.7, and negative at pH values greater than 7.7 [33]. Low TC adsorption efficiency was noted at pH 2 and 3 owing to the electrostatic repulsion between the positively charged NCFW-4 and TC. However, an increase in the pH of 4–7 enhanced the adsorption capacity owing to the neutralization of the TC [33], whereas NCFW-4 remained positively charged. At pH 8, the adsorption capacity considerably increased because of the electrostatic attraction between the negatively charged TC and positively charged NCFW-4 [34]. However, at pH 9, the adsorption capacity was reduced by the change in the electrostatic interaction from strong attraction to strong repulsion as the TC became negatively charged, and the NCFW-4 charge changed from positive to negative. Therefore, a pH of 8 was determined to be the optimal value to achieve the maximum adsorption capacity, and was chosen for the subsequent adsorption experiments.

The TC adsorption capacity of NCFW-4 at four different temperatures (20, 30, 40, and 50℃) is depicted in Fig. 3e. The adsorption capacity increased with the temperature, indicating an endothermic adsorption process [35]. The adsorption capacity of NCFW-4 at 50℃ was slightly higher than that at 20℃. In terms of practical applicability, the temperature of 20℃ was selected for further experiments.

The impact of the initial TC concentration (C0) on the adsorption capacity of the UW, DW, and NCFW-4 was investigated (Fig. 3f). NCFW-4 exhibited the highest adsorption capacity owing to its 3D porous structure and loading of CDs. The construction of the 3D porous structure in NCFW-4 increased its specific surface area, thereby improving its adsorption ability. Meanwhile, the loading of CDs enhanced the amino group-induced adsorption of TC, thereby improving its TC removal efficiency. The sorption isotherm data of NCFW-4 was analyzed using the Langmuir and Freundlich models (Fig. 3h and 3i and Table S1). The Langmuir model was found to be more suitable for describing the TC adsorption of NCFW-4. The theoretical maximum adsorption TC capacity of NCFW-4f was calculated to be 99.17 mg/g [36]. Finally, a comparison with other TC adsorbents, as shown in Table 1 , confirmed the effectiveness of NCFW-4 in removing TC from wastewater.

The kinetics of the TC sorption by the UW, DW, and NCFW-4 were analyzed. As shown in Fig. 3g, the NCFW-4 exhibited the highest adsorption rate. To further elucidate on the sorption kinetics of NCFW-4, two common models were used to fit the sorption kinetics data. From the fitting results (Fig. 3j and 3 k, and Table S2), the pseudo-second-order model provided a better fit for the adsorption of NCFW-4 [35]. These results suggested that the rate-limiting step for the TC sorption of NCFW-4 could be considered chemical adsorption, which was consistent with the findings of previous studies [44].

In the context of practical applications, the reusability of the adsorbent is a critical consideration [45]. In this study, the reusability of NCFW-4 for TC removal was assessed through multiple adsorption–desorption cycles. Prior to each cycle, 0.3 g NCFW-4 sample was pretreated by soaking in 150 mL sodium hydroxide solution (0.15 M) for 3 h, followed by rinsing with deionized water for five times. As shown in Fig. 4 a, NCFW-4 maintained an adsorption capacity of 13.95 mg/g throughout the five cycles which was 80.9% of the initial value. The slight decrease in the adsorption capacity was attributed to the partial and irreversible occupation of sorption sites by TC and minimal mass loss of NCFW-4 during adsorption, desorption, and cyclic washing. The structural integrity of NCFW-4 was confirmed using various analytical techniques, including SEM, FTIR, and XRD. The SEM image (Fig. S3) showed that the 3D porous structure of NCFW-4 remained unchanged after five cycles [46]. The FTIR spectra (Fig. 4b) exhibited comparable chemical groups of reused NCFW-4 to those of initial NCFW-4. Furthermore, the XRD analysis (Fig. 4c) revealed the stable crystal structure of NCFW-4 [6]. All these findings suggest the promising potential of NCFW-4 for practical applications owing to its excellent regeneration and reusability.

In the development of effective sensors, sensitivity and selectivity are paramount considerations [47]. In this study, the selectivity of NCFW-4 in detecting TC was evaluated by assessing its fluorescence response to other antibiotics, such as chloramphenicol (CAP), ofloxacin (OFX), sulfadiazine (SD), TC, enrofloxacin (ENR), oxytetracycline (OTC), ciprofloxacin (CIP), and sulfamethazine (SMZ). As presented in Fig. 5 a, the fluorescence of NCFW-4 was significantly quenched by TC, whereas it remained largely unaffected by other antibiotics. Furthermore, changes in the AQY of NCFW-4 with the addition of these antibiotics were analyzed. No notable reductions were observed, except in the case of TC, as demonstrated in Fig. 5b. These results provided strong evidence of the remarkable selectivity of NCFW-4 for detecting TC.

This sensitivity of NCFW-4 for detecting TC was obtained by measuring its AQY and fluorescence intensity in response to varying TC concentrations. The fluorescence color of NCFW-4 transitioned from blue to black with increasing TC concentrations, as depicted in Fig. 6 a. The AQY of NCFW-4 varied linearly with the TC concentration of 5–200 mg/L. The limit of detection (LOD) determined by the three-standard-deviation method was 2.1 mg/L [48]. Furthermore, with increase in the TC concentration, the fluorescence spectra of NCFW-4 showed a decrease in fluorescence intensity at 470 nm, as depicted in Fig. 6b. A linear correlation was established between F0/F-1 and the TC concentration with a linear range of 0.2–1.0 μg/L and LOD of 0.09 μg/L (R2 = 0.99114). A comparative analysis of the detection performance of NCFW-4 with previously reported fluorescent probes in literature was summarized in Table 2 , which demonstrated that NCFW-4 had a wider linear range and lower LOD.

The efficacy of NCFW-4 in detecting TC in actual water samples obtained from an artificial lake and water pipe situated at the Central South University of Forestry and Technology was evaluated. The quality parameters of the actual water samples were listed in Table S3. Initial attempts to detect TC using NCFW-4 and inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry failed to identify the presence of TC in the water samples. Therefore, the standard addition method was employed in conjunction with NCFW-4 [58]. The results of this analysis were highly precise, with recoveries of 98.86–102.57% and standard deviations of less than 3% (Table S4). These findings suggest that NCFW-4 can be considered a reliable approach for the determination of TC in water samples.

To assess the cytotoxicity of NCFW-4 in NIH/3T3 cells, the CCK-8 assay method was utilized. As shown in Fig. 7 a, the viability of the NIH/3T3 cells remained above 90% when exposed to NCFW-4 and UW, even at a concentration of 1280 μg/mL. These results indicate that NCFW-4 is non-toxic and does not contain any harmful compounds, thereby confirming its suitability for further environmental applications [59].

To further evaluate the potential cytotoxicity of NCFW-4, a live–dead staining assay was performed [20]. As illustrated in Fig. 7b, live cells were labeled with Hoechst 33342, which emits blue fluorescence, whereas the dead cells were stained with propidium iodide (PI), which emit red fluorescence. After 48 h, the majority of the NIH/3T3 cells exposed to NCFW-4 remained viable.

To examine the sorption mechanism of NCFW-4, FTIR spectroscopy was employed. As depicted in Fig. 8 a, the results exhibited a reduction of the peak at 3336 cm−1, corresponding to the N − H and O − H stretching vibrations after TC sorption [60]. Simultaneously, the peak intensity corresponding to the − COOH stretching vibration at 1563 cm−1 was augmented. These spectral modifications imply the involvement of hydroxyl, amino, and carboxyl groups in the sorption process of TC [61].

The sorption mechanism of NCFW-4 was further investigated by XPS. As depicted in Fig. 8b, the XPS survey scan displayed three peaks at 285, 400, and 533 eV, corresponding to C1s, N1s, and O1s, respectively [62]. As shown in Fig. 8c, the analysis of the high-resolution O1s XPS spectrum of NCFW-4 before TC adsorption revealed two component peaks at 532.4 and 531.3 eV, which belonged to the C–O and C = O functional groups, respectively [63]. These peaks shifted to higher binding energies after TC adsorption, indicating hydrogen bonding between –COOH and TC. The N1s spectrum of NCFW-4 prior to TC adsorption (Fig. 8d) showed three component peaks at 398.8, 399.7, and 400.9 eV, corresponding to –N=, –NH2, and –NH3 +, respectively. The increase in the relative intensity of the –NH3 + peak after TC adsorption was due to the formation of –NH3 + through the interaction with amino groups [64]. These findings suggest that both electrostatic and hydrogen bonding interactions contributed to the TC adsorption by NCFW-4.

To investigate the TC detection mechanism of NCFW-4, an examination of UV–vis absorption spectrum of TC was conducted. The UV–vis absorption band of TC significantly overlapped with the fluorescence excitation band of NCFW-4, as depicted in Fig. 9 a. This suggested the involvement of either fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) or inner filter effect (IFE) in the detection process [63]. Furthermore, the fluorescence decay curves of NCFW-4 were obtained before and after TC adsorption, as shown in Fig. 9b-d. The corresponding average lifetimes were calculated as 5.2 and 5.1 ns, respectively. The relatively constant average lifetimes of NCFW-4 before and after TC addition indicated the absence of FRET in this detection system [63]. Therefore, the highly efficient IFE was considered the primary mechanism of TC detection by NCFW-4.

After conducting experiments and characterizing the material, a proposed mechanism for the TC adsorption and detection of NCFW-4 is illustrated in Fig. 10 . Initially, TC accumulation on the NCFW-4 surface was attributed to the electrostatic attraction between the negatively charged TC and the protonated amine groups of NCFW-4. The 3D porous structure of NCFW-4 facilitated the TC diffusion from the surface to the interior of NCFW-4, providing numerous channels for TC transport. The hydrogen bonding and electrostatic interactions between TC and NCFW-4 effectively adsorbed a significant amount of TC during the diffusion process, resulting in a high adsorption capacity for TC. Furthermore, the porous structure of NCFW-4 prevented the aggregation of CDs and stabilized the fluorescence signal. The binding of TC to CDs promoted the IFE, which significantly decreased the fluorescence of NCFW-4.

After estimating the costs for each stage and overall cost of the preparation of NCFW-4, as well as the overall cost, the cost of this adsorbent was compared to that of activated carbon (AC) using cost-estimation procedures reported in the literature [65]. The results (summarized in Table S5) indicated that the total cost for producing 1 kg NCFW-4 was CNY 124.61, whereas 1 kg AC could be purchased at CNY 253.00 from J&K Scientific Co., Ltd. (China). Based on the cost analysis presented in Table S6, NCFW-4 was cost-effective than AC. Moreover, the cost for both adsorbents to remove 1 g TC was evaluated based on their adsorption capacity. Table S5 showed that the cost of removing 1 g TC by NCFW-4 (CNY 1.26) was significantly lower than that by AC (CNY 5.74). The higher adsorption capacity of NCFW-4 for TC was the main factor for this cost disparity. Thus, with its environmental and economic advantages, NCFW-4 has strong potential to be an effective adsorbent for TC removal.

This paper described the successful development of a novel NCFW with exceptional adsorption efficiency and detection sensitivity for the removal and detection of TC. The NCFW architecture was characterized by the 3D porous structure, which allowed TC molecules to readily access and bind to the internal adsorption sites. The TC adsorption process by the NCFW was determined to be a monolayer chemisorption process with a maximum adsorption capacity of 99.17 mg/g. In particular, the mechanism for the TC adsorption by the NCFW was attributed to a combination of hydrogen bonding and electrostatic interactions. Moreover, the NCFW exhibited the detection limit of 0.09 μg/L and linear ranges of 0.2–1.0 μg/L and 5–200 mg/L, making it highly selective and sensitive for TC determination. Therefore, the NCFW has strong potential for a wide range of applications in wastewater treatment, particularly for TC removal and detection.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 31890772), Changsha Natural Science Foundation (Grant No. kq2208414), Hunan Youth Science and Technology Innovation Talent Project (Grant No. 2020RC3043), Training Plan of Young Backbone Teachers in Colleges and Universities of Hunan Province, China (Grant No. 71), General Project by the Department of Education of Hunan Province (Grant No. 21C056) and Hunan Institute of Engineering Talent Research Project (Grant No. 09001003-22013). The authors would like to thank Jialing Wu from Shiyanjia Lab (www.shiyanjia.com) for the XPS analysis.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cej.2023.143284.

ce:section.ce:para:
